title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
text:
SMU Student Startup
Business Plan Competition
2027



📅 Open: 1 Jan 2027


🗓 Close: 31 Mar 2027


🏆 Results: 30 Jun 2027



Organized by Singapore Management University · Modelled after LKYGBPC


Welcome to the Executive Summary for the SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027.
This presentation covers five core operational tasks the organizing team must execute, and for each task we explain both the conventional human approach and a proposed agentic AI approach.
The competition is modelled closely on the Lee Kuan Yew Global Business Plan Competition run by SMU, and all venue and funding is provided by the University.
Key headline numbers: we are targeting 100 finalists, evaluated by 10 judges, with 10 winners announced on 30 June 2027. Applications run for three months from 1 January to 31 March 2027.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
text:
SMU Student Startup
Business Plan Competition
2027



📅 Open: 1 Jan 2027


🗓 Close: 31 Mar 2027


🏆 Results: 30 Jun 2027



Organized by Singapore Management University · Modelled after LKYGBPC


Welcome to the Executive Summary for the SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027.
This presentation covers five core operational tasks the organizing team must execute, and for each task we explain both the conventional human approach and a proposed agentic AI approach.
The competition is modelled closely on the Lee Kuan Yew Global Business Plan Competition run by SMU, and all venue and funding is provided by the University.
Key headline numbers: we are targeting 100 finalists, evaluated by 10 judges, with 10 winners announced on 30 June 2027. Applications run for three months from 1 January to 31 March 2027.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
text:
Competition at a Glance




100
Global Finalists


10
Expert Judges


10
Winners Selected


3 months
Application Window



Eligibility — open worldwide to:

University students (UG, PG, doctoral) currently enrolled
Alumni who graduated within the last 5 years (2022–2027)
Teams of up to 5 members; at least 1 must be the lead founder




Competition Timeline



Period
Milestone




Oct–Dec 2026
Outreach & judge recruitment


1 Jan 2027
Applications open


31 Mar 2027
Applications close


Apr–May 2027
Pitch deck evaluation


Jun 2027
Deliberation & results


30 Jun 2027
Public announcement






This slide gives the essential competition parameters to orient the audience before we dive into the five tasks.
On eligibility: the student/alumni rule is strictly enforced. Applications are submitted as teams; the lead founder must be a current student or recent graduate. All team members must meet the eligibility criteria — you cannot have one eligible founder and four ineligible co-founders.
On timeline: note that judge recruitment and participant outreach both begin well before the January 1 application opening. Waiting until January to recruit judges would be too late — senior figures need months of lead time.
The competition will accept applications from students and teams worldwide, not just Singapore. This global scope is one of the competition’s key differentiators.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
section: Five Core Tasks & The Dual Approach
text:
Five Core Tasks & The Dual Approach










#
Task
Human approach
Agentic AI approach




1
Sourcing participants
University outreach, social media, ambassador programme
AI prospect finder, personalised email drafter, pace monitor


2
Sourcing judges
3-wave invitation process, formal letters, follow-up cadence
Candidate researcher, letter drafter, follow-up scheduler


3
Matching judges to participants
Manual sector tagging, conflict check, chief judge approval
Sector tagger, COI detector, constraint-optimiser


4
Evaluating pitch decks
Calibration session, async scoring portal, anomaly review
Deck summariser, anomaly detector, progress tracker


5
Choosing top 10 winners
Score ranking, deliberation panel, secret ballot
Ranking engine, deliberation briefs, comms bundle





🔑 Core principle: AI handles research, drafting, tracking, and computation. All consequential decisions remain with humans.


This is the structural overview slide. Each of the five tasks has two approaches — Part A is what the organizing team does manually and with conventional tools; Part B is how agentic AI can automate or augment each step.
A key framing point for the audience: this is not an either-or choice. The agentic AI approach is additive — it removes the tedious, time-intensive groundwork (prospect research, follow-up scheduling, matching optimization) so the human team can focus on judgment calls and relationship management.
The core principle is non-negotiable: the AI never makes a decision that determines who participates, who judges, or who wins. Every consequential output — invitation sent, finalist confirmed, winner selected — requires explicit human approval.
In the following slides, we will go through each task in detail.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
text:
Task 1 · Sourcing Participants

Attracting 300–500 global applicants to select 100 finalists
Outreach begins October 2026 — three months before applications open


This is the Task 1 divider slide. The headline target is 300 to 500 applications, which gives a healthy funnel to select 100 high-quality finalists.
A common mistake is waiting until the application portal opens before starting outreach. By then you have already lost months of runway. The organizing team should begin university outreach and ambassador recruitment in October 2026 at the latest.
The next two slides cover the human and AI approaches in detail.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
text:
Task 1 · Part A: Human Approach


Channel 1 — University networks
Email innovation office directors at 200 target universities globally. Provide a ready-to-use promo kit: one-pager, email template, social copy, poster. Assign each university a unique referral code.
Channel 2 — Startup ecosystems
Engage BLOCK71, Antler, SGInnovate, Y Combinator Startup School, Techstars, and regional hubs in Jakarta, Bangalore, Lagos, and São Paulo.

Channel 3 — Digital & social
Post on LinkedIn, Reddit (r/startups), F6S, and Telegram startup channels. Optional paid ads: S$3–8K targeting student founders.
Channel 4 — Student ambassadors
Recruit 10–15 ambassadors. Each hosts a campus info session and recruits peers via a personal referral link. Recognition: SMU IIE Director letter + named on competition website.


⚑ Guardrail: Track application sources from day one. If a channel underperforms by February 2027, redirect effort immediately — do not wait until the application close.


The four channels work in parallel — not sequentially. Do not wait for Channel 1 to complete before starting Channel 2.
The university promo kit is critical: make it zero-effort for the university contact to share. Include a pre-written email, social media captions for LinkedIn and Instagram, and an A3 printable poster. The easier you make it, the higher the pass-through rate.
The student ambassador programme is the highest-ROI channel because peer-to-peer recruitment is the most credible form of outreach for student founders. An email from a fellow student saying “I applied to this and it’s worth your time” converts better than any institutional email from SMU.
On referral codes: assign a unique UTM code or referral ID to every channel and every ambassador. This allows the team to see real-time conversion rates and reallocate effort if one channel is underperforming.
Roadshows: run 4–6 live webinars across time zones in November and December 2026. Record them and post on the competition website — this gives ongoing passive discovery value.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
section: Task 1 · Part B: Agentic AI Approach
text:
Task 1 · Part B: Agentic AI Approach

Research Agent → Outreach Agent → Human Approval → Pipeline Monitor




AI Agent University prospect finder
Crawls 200 university portals, extracts contact names and emails, ranks by QS ranking and known startup activity. Output: structured spreadsheet ready for outreach. ~2 hrs vs. 2 weeks manually.


AI Agent Personalised email drafter
Generates tailored invitations per contact, referencing the university’s known startup focus. Flags uncertain cases for human review before any send.



AI Agent Social listening agent
Monitors LinkedIn and X for student founders who have not yet applied. Drafts DM templates for human review — never sends autonomously.


Automated Eligibility pre-screener
Auto-checks student status, documents, and team size on each submission. Auto-rejects clear fails with a polite email; flags edge cases for human review within 48 hrs.



⚑ Guardrail: No outreach email is sent without explicit human approval. Estimated time saved: ~120 staff-hours over the 3-month application window.


The agentic AI approach does not replace the four channels — it accelerates the most time-consuming parts of executing those channels.
The University Prospect Finder is the biggest time-saver. Manually researching 200 universities, finding the right contact (not just a generic admissions email), and building a clean spreadsheet would take a staff member 10–15 working days. The AI does this in about 2 hours with equivalent or better quality.
The personalised email drafter is important because generic blast emails have very low conversion rates with busy university administrators. A letter that specifically says “given your school’s strong CleanTech programme” is much more likely to get forwarded. The AI drafts these at scale; a human approves a batch each morning.
The eligibility pre-screener reduces the burden on the operations team significantly. In the first two weeks of applications, you will likely receive hundreds of incomplete or ineligible submissions. Auto-handling the obvious cases means staff only deal with genuine edge cases.
One critical point: the social listening agent surfaces warm leads, but a human must review the DM draft before it goes out. Cold outreach on LinkedIn from an SMU account needs to feel genuine, not automated.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
text:
Task 2 · Sourcing Judges

Confirm 10 judges by inviting 25–30 candidates
Start recruitment September 2026 — expect 35–40% acceptance from senior figures


Task 2 covers judge recruitment. The acceptance rate from senior VCs and C-suite executives is typically 35 to 40 percent, which means you need to invite 25 to 30 candidates to reliably land 10 confirmed judges.
Starting in September 2026 is non-negotiable. Q4 is peak conference and board season for senior industry figures. If you wait until November or December, their calendars are already full and you will struggle to get commitments.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
text:
Task 2 · Part A: Human Approach


Four judge profiles to target


VCs & Investors (4–5 slots)
Partners at Sequoia SEA, Monk’s Hill, Wavemaker, East Ventures, Antler


Corporate Leaders (3–4 slots)
CTOs & Chief Innovation Officers at MNCs with Singapore presence — source from SMU IIE corporate partner list first


Successful Founders (2–3 slots)
Series A+ or exited founders — SMU alumni founders are highest-priority contacts


Academics & Policy (1–2 slots)
SMU/NUS professors + optional EDB, A*STAR, or SGInnovate representative



Invitation cadence

Wave 1 (Sep 2026): 15 candidates on SMU letterhead, signed by the Dean
Wave 2 (Oct 2026): 10 more if fewer than 8 accepted
Wave 3 (Nov 2026): Fill remaining slots from reserve list

Each letter must state clearly:
Async deck review (~3–4 hrs total) + one 3-hour deliberation panel in June 2027
Diversity target:
≥40% women judges · ≥5 countries represented
Always maintain 5 confirmed backup judges


The four profile types give you a well-rounded panel. VCs bring commercial lens and market sizing rigour. Corporate innovators bring implementation and enterprise context. Successful founders bring empathy for the founder experience and team assessment skills. Academics and policy experts add credibility and can assess technical feasibility, particularly for deep-tech entries.
The invitation letter is one of the most important documents in the whole competition. It must be on official SMU letterhead and signed by a senior leader — not a generic email from a staff member. The letter should be personalised: one or two sentences explaining specifically why this person’s expertise matters for this competition’s focus.
Being transparent about time commitment is essential. Senior people will not commit to something vague. Tell them exactly: approximately 3 to 4 hours for async deck review over 4 weeks, plus a 3-hour deliberation panel in June 2027.
The reserve list of 5 confirmed backups is non-negotiable. Even judges who accepted 6 months ago will sometimes cancel in the week before the panel due to travel, health, or work crises. A reserve judge who is already briefed and on standby is far better than a last-minute scramble.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
section: Task 2 · Part B: Agentic AI Approach
text:
Task 2 · Part B: Agentic AI Approach



AI Agent Judge candidate researcher
Searches LinkedIn, Crunchbase, and conference speaker databases for candidates matching the 4 profiles. Scores each 1–10 for relevance. Generates 30 ranked candidates in under 2 hours.


AI Agent Invitation letter drafter
Composes personalised letters referencing the candidate’s specific expertise and stating the time commitment precisely. Every draft requires human approval before sending.


Automated Follow-up scheduler
Day 10: auto follow-up email. Day 20: flags for personal LinkedIn message. After 2 no-responses: marks as lapsed, advances next candidate.



AI Agent Diversity gap analyser
Tracks gender, nationality, and domain coverage in real time. Flags imbalances and suggests specific reserve candidates to improve panel balance.


Automated Onboarding packet dispatcher
On acceptance: auto-sends NDA (DocuSign), calendar invite for June panel, portal login, and briefing PDF. Zero manual coordination required for paperwork.



⚑ Guardrail: No invitation letter is sent without explicit human approval. Outreach that misrepresents the competition to a senior executive can permanently damage SMU’s reputation.


The biggest time saving in Task 2 is the Judge Candidate Researcher. Building a list of 30 qualified candidates across the 4 profiles, with their current role, investment thesis, and contact information, would take a partnerships manager 3 to 5 working days. The AI produces this in about 2 hours.
The Invitation Letter Drafter is valuable but requires careful oversight. Every letter must be reviewed by the partnerships manager before sending. The key thing the AI does is handle the research and first draft — the human’s job is to check the personalisation is accurate and that the tone is right for the specific individual.
The Follow-up Scheduler removes the cognitive overhead of tracking a pipeline of 30 people in various states of “invited, pending, declined, lapsed.” The agent handles this automatically and only surfaces cases that need a human decision.
The Diversity Gap Analyser is proactive insurance. Rather than realising at the end that your panel is 9 men from Singapore, it flags this in real time and says “here are two women from your reserve list who would rebalance the panel.”

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
text:
Task 3 · Matching Judges to Participants

Domain-aligned, conflict-free assignment with balanced workloads
Executed in April 2027 after the 100 finalists are confirmed


Task 3 is the matching problem: how do you assign 10 judges to 100 startups in a way that maximises domain expertise alignment, eliminates conflicts of interest, and keeps workloads fair?
Doing this manually in a spreadsheet takes 2 to 3 days and still produces a suboptimal result. This is the task where agentic AI delivers the clearest operational advantage.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
text:
Task 3 · Part A: Human Approach


1 Tag all 100 startups — Assign up to 3 sector tags from a fixed taxonomy: HealthTech · CleanEnergy · AgriTech · EdTech · FinTech · MobilityTech · DeepTech · B2B SaaS · Social Impact · Other


2 Tag all judges — Map each judge to 2–3 domain expertise areas using the same taxonomy. Note preferred deck load (10 or 20).


3 Group into 10 panels of 10 — Cluster startups by shared sector tag. Assign one primary (domain-matched) + one secondary (generalist) judge per panel.


4 Run conflict-of-interest check — Send judges the finalist list. Ask them to flag any investment, advisory role, or personal connection. Reassign flagged startups to an alternate judge.


5 Chief judge approval & portal activation — Chief judge reviews the matrix. On approval, portals unlock with only each judge’s assigned decks visible.



⚑ Guardrail: Overflow management — if 30+ startups share one sector but only 1 judge has that expertise, distribute to generalist judges with a one-page sector guide.


The key design principle is that every startup gets scored by exactly 2 independent judges. This dual-scoring design is essential for two reasons: it reduces individual bias (one judge who happens to dislike a certain business model can’t single-handedly eliminate a team), and it flags anomalies when the two judges’ scores diverge significantly.
The conflict of interest check must happen before the matching matrix is finalised. Judges may not volunteer conflicts unless specifically asked. The process is to send them the full list of 100 finalist names and universities (no decks yet), and ask them to flag any team they have invested in, advised, or know personally.
The sector taxonomy must be fixed in advance and consistently applied. If you let startups self-tag their sectors, you will get misclassification and gaming. Tags should be assigned by the operations team from reading the submitted pitch deck.
The chief judge’s role at the end of this process is to review the proposed matrix, check for anything that looks odd, and give explicit sign-off. This is a governance checkpoint, not a rubber stamp.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
section: Task 3 · Part B: Agentic AI Approach
text:
Task 3 · Part B: Agentic AI Approach



AI Agent Pitch deck sector tagger
Reads all 100 decks. Extracts problem statement, solution, and market section. Assigns sector tags with confidence scores. Flags <70% confidence for human review before matching runs.


AI Agent Conflict-of-interest detector
Fetches each judge’s investment portfolio (Crunchbase) and advisory roles (LinkedIn). Produces a 10×100 conflict matrix: “clear”, “potential”, or “flagged” per cell.



AI Agent Matching optimiser
Constraint-satisfaction algorithm: maximise domain match score, ensure 2 judges per startup, eliminate all conflicts, balance load (8–22 decks per judge). Runs in under 2 hours.


Automated Portal access provisioner
On chief judge approval: automatically unlocks each judge’s portal with exactly their assigned decks. Sends personalised email with assignment list and 4-week deadline.



⏱ Time saving: What takes a human 2–3 days of spreadsheet work (tagging 100 decks, checking 1,000 conflict pairs, balancing 10 judges) takes the agentic system under 2 hours — with a cleaner audit trail.


The Matching Optimiser is the centrepiece of the AI approach for this task. It runs a constraint-satisfaction or linear assignment algorithm that simultaneously satisfies four constraints: domain match maximised, dual coverage for every startup, no conflicts, and load balance. A human trying to satisfy all four constraints manually in a spreadsheet will inevitably make trade-offs and miss edge cases.
The Conflict-of-Interest Detector is important because judges may not be aware of all their conflicts — for example, a junior portfolio company they invested in two years ago that they have mentally stopped tracking. Checking Crunchbase and LinkedIn portfolios systematically catches cases that a self-declaration form would miss.
The Portal Access Provisioner eliminates a manual coordination step that seems simple but is actually error-prone: ensuring each judge can only see their assigned decks and not anyone else’s. This is both a fairness and a confidentiality requirement.
The human approval gate before the portal activates is non-negotiable. The chief judge must review the conflict report, the domain match scores, and the diversity balance report before clicking approve.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
text:
Task 4 · Evaluating Pitch Decks

4-week async scoring window · 100-point rubric · dual independent judges
April – May 2027 — all evaluation is asynchronous to respect judges’ time


Task 4 is the core evaluation process. All scoring is asynchronous — judges review decks at their own pace within a 4-week window. This eliminates the need to coordinate 10 senior people across time zones for synchronous review sessions.
The two design principles are: every startup gets 2 independent scores, and every score comes from a human judge — never from AI.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
text:
Task 4 · Part A: Human Approach


Scoring rubric — 100-point scale



Criterion
Weight
Score




Solution & innovation
25%
1–5


Problem & market
20%
1–5


Business model
20%
1–5


Traction & validation
15%
1–5


Team
15%
1–5


Pitch quality
5%
1–5



Each criterion: mandatory comment (≥1–2 sentences referencing specific deck content)

Process steps

Calibration session — 60-min Zoom briefing for all judges. Walk through rubric + 3 anonymised sample decks at different quality tiers. Recorded for those who cannot attend.
4-week scoring window — Each judge reviews 10–20 decks at ~12–15 min each. Judges cannot see each other’s scores.
Weekly tracking — Reminders at week 2 if below 50%; personal escalation at week 3 if below 70%.
Anomaly review — If two judges’ weighted scores differ by >15 points, chief judge reviews both scoresheets.



The calibration session is one of the most important investments in the evaluation process. Without calibration, you get systematic differences in how judges interpret the rubric — one judge’s “4 on traction” might equal another judge’s “2 on traction.” Even a 60-minute session with sample decks dramatically reduces this variance.
On the rubric design: Solution & Innovation is weighted highest at 25% because this competition wants to reward genuine novelty, not just well-packaged existing ideas. Pitch quality is weighted lowest at 5% deliberately — a brilliant idea in a rough deck should not be penalised excessively. Student founders are not professional pitch coaches.
The mandatory comment field is both a quality control mechanism and a deliverable for participants. Every finalist will receive their scores and the judges’ comments as personalised feedback after the results. If comments are generic (“good team, interesting idea”), that feedback is worthless. The comment field requires specific references to the deck content.
The anomaly threshold of 15 points (on a 100-point scale) is the trigger for chief judge review. This is not necessarily a problem — two domain experts can legitimately disagree significantly on a startup’s prospects. The chief judge review is to understand the disagreement, not to override one judge.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
section: Task 4 · Part B: Agentic AI Approach
text:
Task 4 · Part B: Agentic AI Approach



AI Agent Deck summariser
Generates a 1-page structured summary per deck: problem, solution, market, business model, traction, team. Labelled “AI-generated — orientation only.” Cuts judge prep from ~5 min to ~1 min per deck.


AI Agent Score normalisation engine
If systematic bias detected (e.g. judge whose scores range only 50–70), optionally applies per-judge z-score normalisation. Chief judge approves or rejects before rankings run.



AI Agent Scoring anomaly detector
After all scores submitted: (1) inter-rater gap >15 pts flagged; (2) score compression (range <20 pts) flagged; (3) statistical outliers in top/bottom 5% identified. All sent to chief judge.



Automated Judge progress tracker
Weekly completion reports. Auto-reminder at week 2 if below 50%. Personal escalation at week 3 if below 70%. Routes unfinished decks to reserve judge at window close.


AI Agent Feedback quality reviewer
Checks each comment: ≥10 words, must reference deck content specifically. Prompts judge to elaborate before submission is accepted.



⚑ Critical guardrail: The AI never scores a pitch deck. Scores 1–5 per criterion are entered exclusively by human judges. Enforced at the portal level — no override possible.


The most important thing to communicate on this slide is the guardrail at the bottom: AI never scores. This is a non-negotiable design constraint.
If we allowed AI to produce reference scores — even “just as a starting point” for judges — we would be introducing anchor bias. Judges who see an AI score of 72 before scoring will tend to score near 72, even if their independent judgment would have landed somewhere else. The competition’s integrity depends on truly independent human scoring.
The Deck Summariser is the AI’s most valuable contribution to this task. It does not evaluate — it orientates. A judge opening a pitch deck for the first time spends the first few minutes just understanding what the startup does. The AI pre-processes this and presents it in a consistent format, saving that orientation time and allowing the judge to go straight to evaluating.
The Feedback Quality Reviewer protects the participant experience. If a judge writes “Interesting concept” as their comment, the system will reject the submission and prompt them to be more specific. This ensures that all 100 finalists receive actionable feedback, not boilerplate.
The Progress Tracker is operational insurance. It is surprisingly common for even senior, well-intentioned judges to fall behind on async tasks because their day-to-day work takes over. Automated nudges remove the awkward situation of a staff member having to chase a prominent judge manually.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
text:
Task 5 · Choosing the Top 10 Winners

Quantitative ranking → deliberation panel → secret ballot → 30 Jun 2027 announcement
A two-stage process that combines objective scores with human judgment


Task 5 is the culmination of all the previous work. The winner selection process is designed to combine the objectivity and consistency of quantitative scoring with the qualitative insight that only experienced human judges can provide.
The two-stage design — score ranking to shortlist, then deliberation to final selection — is intentional. Pure quantitative ranking has known limitations: it cannot capture a founder’s tenacity, the strategic value of a business in a particular geography, or the panel’s collective sense that a team can execute despite a weaker deck. The deliberation session allows these judgments.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
text:
Task 5 · Part A: Human Approach


Stage A — Score-ranked longlist (early June)

Compute weighted average from dual-judge scores for all 100 startups
Rank from highest to lowest → top 20 advance to shortlist
Run diversity scan: check if top 20 is skewed by sector, region, or university — flag but do not automatically correct
If score tie at positions 20/21, both advance

Prize structure







Tier
Award




Champion (1st)
Cash + SMU accelerator + mentorship


Excellence (2nd & 3rd)
Cash + co-working credits + investor intros


Top 10
Certificate + website feature + network


Category awards
Best Social Impact, Best DeepTech, People’s Choice




Stage B — Deliberation panel (mid June)

Distribute one-page summaries for top 20 to all judges 3 days before the panel
3-hour session chaired by chief judge
Champion/challenge format: judge who scored highest gets 2 min to advocate; any judge who scored low gets 1 min to challenge
Secret simultaneous ballot — teams with 6+ votes confirmed
Remaining spots filled by composite score + vote count
Chief judge holds casting vote for unresolvable ties
Winners notified privately 5 days before 30 Jun announcement



The diversity scan in Stage A deserves emphasis. It does not change scores — the scoring integrity must be maintained. What it does is ensure the panel enters the deliberation aware of any distributional issues. If 16 of the top 20 are from Singapore, the panel can choose to consider that in deliberation. But the scan informs the choice; it does not make it.
The champion/challenge format in Stage B is borrowed from investment committee practice. It is a disciplined way to surface the strongest arguments for and against borderline teams without the discussion devolving into unfocused debate. The 2-minute / 1-minute time limits are important — enforce them.
The secret simultaneous ballot is important for the same reason as any secret ballot: it prevents anchoring and herd behaviour. If judges reveal their votes sequentially, the later voters are influenced by the earlier ones. Simultaneous revelation means each judge commits to their own independent judgment first.
Announcing all 100 finalists publicly is one of the most important recommendations in this playbook. Every finalist gets a prestigious credential regardless of where they rank in the final 10. This dramatically improves the competition’s reputation and word-of-mouth for future editions.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
section: Task 5 · Part B: Agentic AI Approach
text:
Task 5 · Part B: Agentic AI Approach



AI Agent Ranking & shortlist engine
Computes weighted averages, applies approved normalisation, produces ranked leaderboard and top-20 shortlist with per-criterion score breakdown PDFs for each team.


AI Agent Deliberation brief writer
1-page brief per top-20 team: strongest/weakest criteria, key judge comment quotes, how the team compares to similar startups in the pool. Distributed 3 days before the panel.


AI Agent Diversity & balance analyser
Analyses top 20 across sector, geography, university tier, and gender. Reports imbalances to chief judge before deliberation — informed choice, not a blind one.



Automated Winner communications bundle
Auto-generates: winner notification emails, press release draft, website update copy, LinkedIn/X posts in 3 tones. All require Director sign-off before any are sent.


Automated Feedback report generator
For all 100 finalists: personalised report with per-criterion scores, anonymised judge comments, and relative pool ranking. Distributed on announcement day — impractical to do manually at this scale.



⚑ Critical guardrail: The final top-10 decision is made exclusively by judges via secret ballot. AI ranks and briefs — it never recommends who should win.


The Deliberation Brief Writer is particularly valuable. In a 3-hour panel covering 20 startups, judges are under time pressure. A well-structured 1-page brief that surfaces the key facts — strongest criterion, weakest criterion, how this team compares to similar startups in the pool — enables a higher quality discussion than judges working purely from memory of decks they reviewed weeks ago.
The Feedback Report Generator is the most visible participant-facing AI output in the whole competition. Every finalist gets a personalised report: their scores per criterion, the judges’ comments (anonymised so no judge can be identified), and their ranking context (“top 15% on innovation, top 40% overall”). Producing this manually for 100 finalists would require several days of staff work. The AI produces all 100 in minutes.
The Winner Communications Bundle is operational efficiency: the press release, winner emails, and social media posts all need to be ready to go at the exact moment the embargo lifts on 30 June. The AI drafts everything in advance; the Director reviews and approves. The moment the button is pressed, everything goes out simultaneously.
Repeat the critical guardrail clearly: the AI produced no recommendation about who should win. The ranking is input to the human deliberation — not the output.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
text:
AI Guardrail Summary



✗ AI must NEVER

Send outreach emails or invitations autonomously
Score a pitch deck — scoring is exclusively a human function
Activate the judge–participant matching matrix without chief judge approval
Recommend who the top 10 winners should be
Send winner notifications or press releases without Director sign-off




✓ AI may operate autonomously

Auto-screen clearly eligible or ineligible applications
Send automated progress reminders to judges mid-window
Generate personalised feedback reports for all 100 finalists after results confirmed
Produce weekly application pace reports and dashboards
Draft outreach copy, letters, and comms bundles (all pending human approval)




🔑 Core principle: AI handles research, drafting, tracking, and computation. Every decision that determines who participates, who judges, or who wins is made by humans.


This slide is the governance summary. Print it. Put it on the wall in the operations room.
The two columns represent a clean separation of concerns: the left column is where AI autonomy would undermine the integrity of the competition; the right column is where AI autonomy saves the team significant time without any integrity risk.
The line between the two columns is not arbitrary. The test is: “does this action determine an outcome that affects a participant, a judge, or the competition’s result?” If yes, a human must make that decision explicitly. If no — if it is a research, drafting, reminder, or report generation task — the AI may operate autonomously.
This governance framework should be documented in the competition’s internal operating procedures and shared with any external partners or sponsors who ask about the role of AI in the process.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
text:
Operations & Recommended Tools


Core team structure



Role
Count




Competition Director
1


Marketing & outreach lead
1–2


Judge & partnerships manager
1


Applications & platform manager
1


Ambassador & community coordinator
1


Chief judge (external, senior)
1




Recommended platform stack

F6S / Submittable Applications portal
Airtable Scoring matrix & matching
Notion Operations documentation
Zoom / Teams Panels & judge briefings
Mailchimp Applicant communications
DocuSign Judge NDAs & confirmations
LinkedIn Ads Targeted participant outreach

Compliance requirements

All judge pitch deck access via login-gated portal (confidentiality)
Participant data consent collected in application form
All judges sign NDA before receiving any decks
Singapore PDPA compliance — SMU Legal to review competition T&Cs before 1 Jan 2027 launch



The team structure is lean for what is a fairly complex operation. The key is that each role has a clear owner with no ambiguity. The Competition Director has overall accountability; the Judge & Partnerships Manager owns all judge-related communications; the Applications & Platform Manager owns the technical side of the portal.
The Chief Judge is an external appointment — a senior industry figure, ideally someone with prior judging experience at competitions like LKYGBPC or Hult Prize. Their role is governance and quality control across the evaluation and selection process.
On the platform stack: the recommendation is to use purpose-built competition management tools (F6S, Submittable) rather than building a custom portal. These platforms have battle-tested application workflows, eligibility screening, file upload management, and applicant communication tools. The custom portal route is tempting but typically doubles the operational complexity.
The PDPA compliance point is important for a competition collecting personal data from students worldwide. SMU Legal should review the terms and conditions, privacy policy, and data retention policy before the portal goes live on 1 January 2027. This is not optional — it is a legal requirement.

title: SMU Student Startup Business Plan Competition 2027
text:
Summary & Key Decisions Required


✓ Confirmed by this playbook

Competition structure and eligibility criteria
Five core operational tasks and their workflows
Dual approach: human + agentic AI for each task
AI guardrail framework
Judge profile targets and invitation process
Scoring rubric and evaluation design



→ Decisions required from leadership

Confirm prize structure and funding allocation
Appoint Competition Director and chief judge
Approve agentic AI tool stack and budget
Sign off on PDPA-compliant T&Cs before 1 Jan 2027
Confirm SMU letterhead authority for judge invitation letters
Approve launch communications strategy




Applications open 1 January 2027 · Results announced 30 June 2027
Singapore Management University — Organized by SMU Institute of Innovation & Entrepreneurship


This closing slide sets up the conversation about what the organizing team needs from leadership before the competition can launch.
The left column represents what has been decided and documented. The right column represents genuine open items that require leadership input — these are not things the organizing team can decide unilaterally.
The most time-sensitive items are: appointing the Competition Director (should happen by July 2026 to allow them time to hire the rest of the team), confirming the prize funding (judges and participants both want to know the prize structure before they commit), and getting legal sign-off on the T&Cs (this often takes longer than expected).
The agentic AI tool stack approval is also worth flagging as time-sensitive. If SMU has procurement processes or IT security reviews for new software platforms, these need to begin well in advance of October 2026 when outreach starts.
Thank you — happy to take questions on any of the five tasks or the AI approach in more detail.
